coredata.dc:title: Preparation of carbon dots decorated graphene/polyaniline composites by supramolecular in-situ self-assembly for high-performance supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shaoying
Gao, Aimei
Yi, Fenyun
Shu, Dong
Cheng, Honghong
Zhou, Xiaoping
He, Chun
Zeng, Depeng
Zhang, Fan
coredata.dc:description: Supramolecular in-situ self-assembly is achieved to prepare carbon nanodots decorated graphene/polyaniline composites by adding β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) as the bonding agent in this work. With its unique structure, β-CD can form a supramolecular system with graphene oxide layers, generating the inclusion compound with aniline monomers, which makes the in-situ polymerization of polyaniline on the surface of graphene oxide. In the hydrothermal process, β-CD is carbonized to carbon nanodots anchored on the surface of reduced graphene oxide. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images show that the prepared reduced graphene oxide/carbon nanodots/polyaniline (RGO@CN/PANI) nanocomposites exhibit the micro morphology of a regular polyaniline particle array on the surface of graphene layers. The electrochemical tests demonstrate that the RGO@CN/PANI nanocomposites exhibit high specific capacitance (up to 871.8 F g−1 at 0.2A g−1), low charge transfer resistance (Rct), and presentable cycling performance (72% capacitance retention after 10000 cycles), indicating the satisfied performance for supercapacitors. The promising performance of RGO@CN/PANI can be attributed to the synergistic effect of RGO, carbon nanodots and PANI, as well as the regular microstructure in which the effective reaction area of polyaniline is increased and the rapid ion transport channel is provided.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots modified graphene
Polyaniline
β-cyclodextrin
Supramolecular in situ self-assembly

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology analyses of RGO@CN25/PANI y nanocomposites. SEM/TEM imagine of (a/d) RGO@CN25/PANI8, (b/e) RGO@CN25/PANI10, (c/f) RGO@CN25/PANI12.
XRD patterns of graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (RGO), PANI, and RGO@CN25/PANI8 composite.
Raman pattern of reduced graphene oxide (RGO), PANI and RGO@CN25/PANI8.
FTIR spectra of PANI and RGO@CN25/PANI8.
XPS spectra of RGO@CN25/PANI8: (a) full spectra; (b) N1s spectra; (c) C1s spectra.

Electrochemical properties of PANI, RGO, and RGO@CN25/PANI8 electrodes (vs. Hg/Hg2SO4 in 1 M H2SO4): (a) CV curves of RGO@CN25/PANI8 at different scan rates; (b) CV curves of RGO@CN25/PANI8, PANI, and RGO at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1; (c) GCD curves of RGO@CN25/PANI8 at different current densities; (d) GCD curves of RGO@CN25/PANI8, PANI and RGO at a current density of 0.2 A g−1; (e) Calculated specific capacitance of RGO@CN25/PANI8, PANI, and RGO at different current densities; and (f) Nyquist plots of RGO@CN25/PANI8, PANI, and RGO.
Cycling performance of RGO@CN25/PANI8 and PANI: (a) comparison with polyaniline; (b) capacitance retention of RGO@CN25/PANI8 at a scanning speed of 30 mVs−1 in 5001–10000 cycles.

Electrochemical performance of RGO@CN25/PANI y (y = 4/6/8/10/12). (a) CV curves of RGO@CN25/PANI y at 50 mV s−1; (b) Calculated Specific capacitance at the current density of 0.5 A g−1 (line 1) and Rct of RGO@CN25/PANI y (line 2); (c) Specific capacitance of RGO@CN25/PANI y at different current densities.
Electrochemical performance of the reported graphene/PANI composites prepared with linker additions.

row.entry:
73% after 10000 cycles
94% in 5001–10000 cycles at 30 mV s−1

head.ce:title: Preparation of carbon dots decorated graphene/polyaniline composites by supramolecular in-situ self-assembly for high-performance supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Environment, South China Normal University, Guangzhou, 510006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou, 510275, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Materials and Technology for Electrochemical Energy Storage (Ministry of Education), Guangzhou, 510006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Materials and Technology for Electrochemical Energy Storage (Ministry of Education)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Environment, South China Normal University, Guangzhou, 510006, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Supramolecular in-situ self-assembly is achieved to prepare carbon nanodots decorated graphene/polyaniline composites by adding β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) as the bonding agent in this work. With its unique structure, β-CD can form a supramolecular system with graphene oxide layers, generating the inclusion compound with aniline monomers, which makes the in-situ polymerization of polyaniline on the surface of graphene oxide. In the hydrothermal process, β-CD is carbonized to carbon nanodots anchored on the surface of reduced graphene oxide. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images show that the prepared reduced graphene oxide/carbon nanodots/polyaniline (RGO@CN/PANI) nanocomposites exhibit the micro morphology of a regular polyaniline particle array on the surface of graphene layers. The electrochemical tests demonstrate that the RGO@CN/PANI nanocomposites exhibit high specific capacitance (up to 871.8 F g−1 at 0.2A g−1), low charge transfer resistance (Rct), and presentable cycling performance (72% capacitance retention after 10000 cycles), indicating the satisfied performance for supercapacitors. The promising performance of RGO@CN/PANI can be attributed to the synergistic effect of RGO, carbon nanodots and PANI, as well as the regular microstructure in which the effective reaction area of polyaniline is increased and the rapid ion transport channel is provided.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing demand of clean, sustainable, and renewable energy, electrochemical energy storage devices that have the higher energy density and power density are urgently needed [1–3]. Supercapacitors, also called electrochemical capacitors (ECs), possess higher power density and much longer cycle life than the ordinary secondary batteries. Based on different mechanisms, supercapacitors can be classified into two types: electrochemical double layer capacitors (EDLCs) and pseudocapacitors. Owing to the requirement of charge accumulation between the electrode/electrolyte interfaces, the electrode materials of EDLCs are mainly carbon materials with high surface areas [4,5]. Different from EDLCs, pseudo-capacitors achieve the energy storage by the fast Faradic reactions at the surface of the electrode, which depend on the electrochemically active materials such as transition metal oxides and electrically conductive polymers [6].

As one of the most widely studied EDLC materials, graphene is a monolayer of graphite with a crystalline allotrope of sp2 bonded carbon, which processes favorable chemical stability, high electrical conductivity, and large surface area [7,8]. Moreover, graphene provides excellent cycling stability and high reversibility during the charge/discharge process of EDLCs. However, the specific capacitance of graphene in practical is lower than the theoretical value because of the serious aggregation of graphene nanosheets via strong π-π interactions. To this end, many methods have been used to overcome the above-mentioned drawback, one of which is preparing graphene-conductive polymer composites [9]. As typical pseudo-capacitors materials [7], electrically conductive polymers such as polyaniline (PANI), polypyrrole (Ppy), polythiophene (PTh), and poly(vinylphenylene) that contain large conjugation structures with substantial sp2 hybridized carbons enable the intermolecular charge transport [10–12]. With high specific capacitance, three different oxidation states and low cost, PANI has been regarded as a promising material for supercapacitors [8]. However, the swelling and shrinkage issues of the polymer backbone in the doping/dedoping processes cause poor cyclical stability. Nowadays, a useful approach to reduce the issues is to fabricate the hybrid nanocomposites that contain graphene and PANI [13]. The graphene component of the synthetic composites contributes to the excellent conductivity, mechanical properties, and improved cycle stability; meanwhile, the PANI component provides high pseudocapacitance [14].

In the preparation of graphene/polyaniline composites as the electrode materials for supercapacitors, the binding methods between graphene and polyaniline are briefly classified as follows: (1) Solution blending: Polyaniline and graphene are uniformly dispersed in the system by physical mixing or ultrasonic treatment. This is the most facile method and the relative content of one single component can be accurately controlled. However, without strong interaction between graphene and polyaniline, two components may aggregate respectively, resulting in unsatisfied compound effect [15]. Chang et al. [16] fabricated polyaniline and graphene oxide composites via the mechanical mixing method, which showed a CF value of 437.8 F g−1 at a current density of 4 A g−1. (2) In-situ polymerization: graphene oxide-polyaniline composites are synthesized via an in-situ chemical oxidation polymerization or electropolymerization by adding aniline monomer into graphene oxide dispersion. With abundant functional groups on the surface of graphene oxide, active sites exist for the polymerization of aniline and the surface morphology of in-situ grown polyaniline can be formed. The composites with different polyaniline contents can be obtained by controlling the polymerization conditions. In this method, the uniform dispersion of polyaniline of in-situ polymerization promotes the good combination of polymer and graphene [17]. Zheng et al. [18] fabricated 3D multi-growth site graphene (MSG)/polyaniline (PANI) composites in which polyaniline grew on the surface of graphene as the irregular particles. The MSG/PANI showed improved specific capacitance of 912 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. Qu et al. [19] prepared graphene-coupled polyaniline (GO@PANI) nanosheets by an in-situ polymerization. The pattern of PANI grown on graphene was in the needle shape, and the composites showed the high areal capacitance of 679 mF cm−2. (3) Pre-treatments of graphene oxide before in-situ polymerization: The graphene oxide layer can be modified to increase certain functional groups, from which aniline is tightly attached to the graphene oxide surface with the interaction between functional groups and aniline monomers. Thus, the surface morphology of the composites is more evenly distributed. At the same time, the oxidation functional groups on the surface of graphene oxide can interact with some substances, so the aniline can be connected to the surface of graphene oxide by adding some linking agents, realizing the composites on the molecular level. The prepared polyaniline can also be distributed on the surface of graphene oxide and eventually grown vertically on the graphene layer. At present, CTAB, plasma, p-phenylenediamine (PBA), DAS, PSS and so on are mainly recorded in the literature as the linkers. Through the interaction with linkers, graphene oxide and aniline are connected together in which the charge interaction, chemical bond, π-π force are mainly recorded as the interaction [20,21]. Hu et al. [22] used tannic acid (TA) as a linking agent to fix aniline monomer on the graphene surface and prepared the flexible free-standing reduced graphene oxide (rGO)/PANI nanocomposite films, which showed the high specific capacitance of 0.92 F cm−2 (>1314.3 F cm−3). In addition, the morphology of polyaniline grown in the composites was different depending on the binding mode.

One of the key factors that affect the electrochemical behavior of PANI is the morphology, and altering the polymer morphology during the synthesis can achieve better supercapacitive performance. Herein, graphene/polyaniline composites were prepared via the addition of β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) and the supramolecular self-assembly process. With a hydrophobic inner cavity and a hydrophilic exterior, cyclodextrins (CDs) are cyclic annular oligosaccharides composed of 6, 7, or 8 glucose units (α, β or γ-CD, respectively). It has been reported that β-CD can form hydrogen bonds with graphene oxide [23]. Meanwhile, the hydrophobic cavities of CDs can incorporate into non-water-soluble organic guest molecules. Thus, CDs can form a unique supramolecular inclusion structure with small molecules, fixing aniline on the surface of graphene oxide closely. Polyaniline can grow along the surface of graphene oxide to form regular arrays in the polymerization process. In this work, we successfully prepared the graphene/carbon dots/polyaniline (RGO@CN/PANI) nanocomposites by using this principle. The reduction of graphene oxide was realized by the one-pot hydrothermal reaction, in which the added β-CD was also carbonized to carbon dots that decorated on the surface of the graphene layers and reduced the agglomeration of graphene. The obtained RGO@CN/PANI nanocomposite exhibited the fine capacitance performance (620 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1), satisfactory conductive property, and good cycling performance (72% capacitance retention after 10000 cycles), indicating that the composites prepared by the supramolecular in-situ self-assembly method are suitable for the electrode materials of supercapacitors.

Natural flake graphite(300 meshes), sulphuric acid(H2SO4), chlorhydric acid(HCl), sodium nitrate(NaNO3), mineral chameleon(KMnO4), hydrogen peroxide(H2O2), ammonium persulfate(APS), β-cyclodextrin(β-CD), citric acid, absolute ethyl alcohol, acetylene black, N-Methyl pyrrolidone(NMP), aniline were used as received.

The microstructure morphologies of the materials were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, ZEISS Ultra 55), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-2100HR), and Raman spectrum (inVia). X-ray diffraction (XRD, X'Pert PRO) analyses of the materials were tested by a Bruker X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). The Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR, IRPrestige-21) spectroscopy measurements were investigated by a FTIR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250 XI), was employed to examine changes in the electron binding energy of materials.

The graphene used in this study was produced from graphene oxide (GO) by a hydrothermal reduction, and GO was prepared by modified Hummers methods. Firstly 4 g of graphite and NaNO3 were weighed and mixed with 92 mL concentrated sulphuric acid in a conical flask and magnetically stirred in the 5 °C ice bath. Secondly, 12 g of KMnO4 was added to the mixture slowly and the temperature was kept for 60 min before the reaction. Then, the temperature was set to 38 °C and the reaction system was kept stirring for 1 h. After adding 184 mL of distilled water into the solution drop by drop, the reaction system was heated to 98 °C and kept for 30 min. To remove the excess oxidant, 40 mL of H2O2 (30%) was dropped into the mixture after the dilution of the reaction system with 300 mL of distilled water. Finally, the obtained solution was washed by 30% hydrochloric acid aqueous solutions and distilled water before it was dried in the oven for a night.

The RGO@CN/PANI composites were synthesized by the simple in-situ polymerization and one-pot hydrothermal reduction. 0.489 g of synthesized black GO solid was ground to the powder and suspended in 40 mL of deionized water. The mixture was then ultrasonicated for 2 h to make the sufficient dispersion of the GO nanosheets. Later, few amounts of β-CD were added to the well-dispersed GO colloid and continued the ultrasonic dispersion for 1 h before the adjunction of citric acid. The amount of citric acid was depended on the weight of added aniline. After magnetic stirring for 30 min, the temperature of the system was cooled down to less than 5 °C and few amounts of aniline were dropped into the conical flask. The supermolecule in-situ self-assembly process was controlled to last for 30 min, followed by adding 8 mL of ammonium persulfate solution (solution concentration depended on the amount of aniline) drop-by-drop. The in-situ polymerization was carried out under the ice-bath and non-stirring environment for 1 h to get the dark green gel precursor. The reduction of GO was accomplished by the one-pot hydrothermal reduction at 180 °C for 3 h, and the final product was washed with deionized water and absolute alcohol for three times. The amounts of added β-CD and aniline were studied, in which the additive aniline varied from 4 to 12 mmol while β-CD varied from 0.25 to 0.75 mmol. Depending on their amount, the prepared nanocomposites were named as RGO@CN x /PANI y . (x = 25/50/75, representing the added β-CD amount of 0.25, 0.50 and 0.75 mmol; y = 4/6/8/10/12, representing the added aniline amount of 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 mmol).

The black product was dried at 60 °C for 6 h before using as the electrodes materials. The active materials, acetylene black, and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) were mixed in a mass ratio of 6:2:2 and dispersed in NMP. Then, the dispersed mixture was coated onto the stainless steel sheet on which the mass of the coating material was nearly 1 mg and the coating area was 1 cm2. All electrochemical tests were carried out on a CHI660E work station (CHI, Shanghai) with a three-electrode system which contained the working electrode, platinum sheet as a counter electrode, and the Hg/Hg2SO4 electrode as the reference electrode in 1.0 M H2SO4 aqueous solution. Cyclic voltammogram (CV) measurements were performed at the scanning speed ranging from 2 to 50 mV s−1 under the potential window between −0.7 and +0.3 V. The galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) performance was tested at different current densities ranged from 0.2 to 10 A g−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out in the frequency ranging from 10 kHz to 0.01 Hz.

The specific capacitance (Cs) of the electrodes was calculated by GCD curves according to the following equation
(1)
Cs = It / m Δ V
where I is the constant discharge current (A); t is the discharge time(s); m stands for the mass of active materials in the electrode, and
Δ V
represents the potential window.

With the unique characteristic of β-CD, both graphene oxide and small molecule materials can form the supermolecule system, offering the opportunity for the preparation of the graphene/carbon-dots/polyaniline (RGO@CN/PANI) compound. The RGO@CN/PANI composites were prepared by the supramolecular in-situ self-assembly method as illustrated in Fig. 1 . Because of the rich oxygen-contained surface groups such as OH,COOH, graphene oxide can produce intermolecular hydrogen bond with the hydroxyl on the hydrophilic side of β-CD [24]. Moreover, the external-inner cavity structure of β-CD is similar to the bucket which contains the hydrophobic inner cavity and the hydrophilic external cavity, enabling the formation of unique inclusion structure with many different small molecules due to the hydrophobic-hydrophilic non-Van Edward intermolecular force. Then, aniline is introduced to the graphene oxide/β-CD intermolecular system, which can form the inclusion structure between the lipophilic benzene ring and β-CD [25]. So the graphene oxide/β-CD/aniline intermolecular system was form. When the hydrophobic benzene ring of aniline intercalated with β -CD cavity, the activity of the exposed amino group of aniline increased and the nucleation centers of polyaniline crystal were provided. After adding the polymerization initiator, the polymerization begins with the exposed amino group of the graphene oxide/β-CD/aniline intermolecular system, resulting in the neat growth of PANI along the GO surface. Finally, GO was reduced by the simple one-pot hydrothermal method and cyclodextrin was also carbonated into carbon dots in this process with which serious agglomeration of graphene can be reduced because of the separate effect.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to characterize the surface morphology of the prepared materials, and the SEM images of RGO@CN/PANI composites and other as-prepared materials are shown in Fig. 2, Figs. S2, and S3. From the SEM image of RGO@CN25/PANI8 (Fig. 2 (a)), well-ordered PANI nanoparticle arrays vertically grew on the surfaces of graphene sheets without serious self-agglomeration. Thus, the serious agglomeration of graphene was effectively reduced via this supramolecular in-situ self-assembly method, which increased the specific surface area of the supercapacitor electrode material. Notably, contact areas between the electrolyte solution and the active material increased, which is beneficial for the development of the double layer capacitance of graphene components. To investigate the influence of β-CD on the surface morphology of the composites, the SEM characterization of RGO@CN x /PANI8 (x = 50/75) was performed, as shown in Fig. S1 (a) and (b). The growth of ordered polyaniline arrays was observed on the surface of graphene layers in two composites. In contrast, without the addition of β-CD, RGO/PANI8 was produced with the morphology of amorphous polyaniline covered on the graphene (Fig. S3 (a)), demonstrating the influence of β-CD on the surface morphology of the composites. The contrast between Fig. S3 (b) (CN/PANI, with morphology of polyaniline which was consisted of nanoparticles) and (c) (PANI, with morphology of smooth coral-like polyaniline) further proved that β-CD contributed to the formation of the granular morphology of the polyaniline.

In consideration of the effect of the aniline amount, Fig. 2(a)–(c) and Fig. S2 (a) to (b) show the SEM images of RGO@CN/PANI materials prepared by adding different amounts of aniline (4/6/8/10/12 mmol), respectively. From Fig. 2(a)–(c), it was found that when the aniline amount increased (8–12 mmol), the polyaniline nanoparticles arrays on the graphene layers tended to form dense amorphous state and gradually covered the graphene surface, which resulted in the poor penetration of electrolyte solutions. When the amount of aniline was too low (4–6 mmol), the regular polyaniline arrays could not be formed (Figs. S2(a) and (b)), which could not realize the pseudo-capacitance performance of polyaniline. Hence, the proper amount of aniline is important for the formation of the surface morphology of RGO@CN/PANI, which can improve the capacitive properties of the nanocomposites.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was also used to characterize the microstructure of the prepared materials. Previous studies have proven that β-CD can be carbonized to the carbon dots under the high temperature and pressure in the hydrothermal process during the reduction of graphene oxide [23]. The carbon dots can remain on the surface of graphene to separate the graphene layers and reduce the agglomeration. In the TEM image of RGO@CN25/PANI8 shown in Fig. 2(d), obvious carbon dots were observed on the layers of graphene, indicating the participation of β-CD in the supramolecular system of graphene oxide/β-CD/aniline. Fig. S1(c) (RGO@CN50/PANI8) and (d)(RGO@CN75/PANI8) show the TEM images of materials with different amounts of β-CD, from which the carbon dots modified surface of graphene can also be observed. When the amount of β-CD increase, the distribution of carbon dots on graphene layers become denser. However, Fig. 2(d)–(f) show that the carbon dots on graphene surface decrease when the aniline content increases, which is attributed to the increased thick amorphous polyaniline that covered the graphene surface. By using the one-pot hydrothermal method to reduce graphene oxide and carbonize cyclodextrin simultaneously, the micromorphology integrity of the prepared materials can be effectively maintained and the agglomeration of graphene layers can be reduced.

To sum up, the surface morphology of the RGO@CN/PANI composites observed by SEM/TEM display the characteristics as follows: Firstly, the polyaniline is self-assembled on the surface of graphene, and confined between the graphene layers, which are beneficial to restrain the swelling of polyaniline chain in charge and discharge processes and improve the cycling performance of the composites. Secondly, the carbonized cyclodextrin remains on the surface of graphene, and the formed carbon dots can play a harmonious role with polymer nanoparticles to reduce the self-agglomeration of graphene. Thirdly, polyaniline nanoparticles can grow regularly on the surface of graphene layers with the adjunction of β-CD, helping to improve the wettability of the electrolyte and the effective utilization ratio of the polyaniline.

The XRD patterns of GO, RGO, PANI, and RGO@CN25/PANI8 are presented in Fig. 3 . The XRD pattern of GO shows a sharp and strong peak at around 10°, which is the characteristic peak of graphite oxide, indicating the successful oxidation of crystalline flake graphite by the modified Hummers method. In contrast, no obvious peaks at about 10° are found in the patterns of RGO and RGO@CN25/PANI8, revealing that graphite oxide was completely reduced after the hydrothermal reaction. A broad peak at 25.8° which corresponds to the (002) plane of hexagonal graphitic carbon can be seen in the RGO pattern, indicating the typical graphite structure. The weak peaks at about 42° for GO, RGO, and RGO@CN25/PANI8 are related to the (111) plane of graphite structure, implying the existence of graphene component in the RGO@CN25/PANI8 nanocomposites. PANI shows two smooth characteristic peaks at 20° and 25°, which correspond to the (021) and (200) planes of doped polyaniline salt and chain structure [26,27]. In the pattern of RGO@CN25/PANI8 nanocomposite, a broad peak is observed at 25°, which presents the similar diffraction peaks to those of pure polyaniline, indicating that PANI is successfully deposited on the surface of graphene nanosheets via the supramolecular in-situ self-assembly method.

Raman spectroscopy was used to investigate the structures of the as-obtained samples, and the patterns of RGO, PANI, and RGO@CN25/PANI8 are shown in Fig. 4 . Here RGO displays two sharp diffraction peaks at 1350 and 1582 cm−1 that are generally named D band and G band, respectively. D band of carbon-based materials represents the disordered structure and defects in graphite, corresponding to the transformation of sp2/sp3 hybridized graphitic carbon atoms [28], whereas G band is caused by the propagation of phonons along the graphite structure and represents the perfect sp2 carbon domain (E2g symmetry). As a contrast, PANI was identified with weak typical peaks of 1518 cm−1 (CN quinoid), 1377 cm−1 (ArN), 1332 cm−1 (CN benzenoid), and strong G band at 1573 cm−1 (the CC stretching of the benzene ring) [29]. After the supramolecular self-assembly process, the target RGO@CN25/PANI8 composites present the combined pattern of PANI and RGO, indicating the effective combination of PANI with graphene in RGO@CN25/PANI8 composites. Moreover, the G band of graphene in the RGO@CN25/PANI8 film exhibits the blue shift compared to RGO, suggesting the existence of charge transfer between PANI nanoparticles and RGO and implying their further interactions.

FTIR is commonly used to characterize the elements and functional groups of organic composites. The FTIR spectra of RGO@CN25/PANI8 and PANI are shown in Fig. 5 . The broad band at 3419 cm−1 in RGO@CN25/PANI8 is the characteristic absorption peak of the NH bond in the polymer [30]. The peak caused by the stretching vibrations of the NH bonds indicates the existence of amino-groups in the polymer chain. The broad peaks around 3228 cm−1 are belonging to OH groups which are ascribed to the absorbed moisture of the PANI. The peak at 1583 cm−1 in the curve of PANI is corresponding to the absorption vibration of N = Q = N in quinone structure, and the shift of this peak at 1574 cm−1 in the curve of RGO@CN25/PANI8 indicates the interactions between different components in the composite [31]. Except for the quinone structure, aromatic amines also present another characteristic peak of ArN absorption vibration at 1373, 1392 and 1290 cm−1, which agrees well with the Raman result. In addition, the peaks at 1493 and 1138 cm−1 in both materials are caused by the stretching vibration of the benzenoid ring skeleton and in-plane bending vibration of benzene, respectively. Moreover, the peaks observed at 814 and 839 cm−1 are related to the out-of-plane bending vibrations which are influenced by the substitution of benzene rings. These two kinds of characteristic peaks represent the disubstituted benzene, indicating that the polymerization of aniline is in the form of head and tail connection. By comparing the FTIR spectra of RGO@CN25/PANI8 and PANI, the spectrum of RGO@CN25/PANI8 nanocomposites is similar with that of PANI, revealing the existence of PANI component in the nanocomposites, and the shift of several characteristic peaks indicates the composite (RGO@CN/PANI) on the molecular level was achieved.

To further analyze the elemental composition and chemical bonding of the prepared materials, XPS measurements were employed. It can be observed from Fig. 6 (a) that C, N, and O elements are detected in the full spectrum of RGO@CN25/PANI8 composites as expected. The N 1s peaks at about 400 eV in Fig. 6(a) indicate the existence of nitrogen element in RGO@CN25/PANI8 and demonstrate the existence of PANI component in the nanocomposites, which are consistent with the results obtained from the Raman and FTIR spectra. Three Gaussian peaks in the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 6(c)) with the binding energy of 284.1 (CC bonds of aromatic or aliphatic), 284.7 eV (CO bonds) and 285.9 eV (CN bonds) can further confirm the existence of polyaniline in the composites. The analysis of the surface atomic ratio shows that the contents of nitrogen in pure PANI (without hydrothermal treatment, shown in Fig. S4) and RGO@CN25/PANI8 are 13.55% and 4.19%, respectively, from which the content of polyaniline in the RGO@CN25/PANI8 composites can be estimated to about 30%. In contrast, the surface atomic ratio of O in the RGO@CN25/PANI8 composites is higher than that of pure PANI, owing to the remained oxygen functional groups after the hydrothermal reduction of graphene oxide. The deconvolutions of the N 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 6(c)) for RGO@CN25/PANI8 show three peaks at 398.5, 399.8, and 400.5 eV, which are associated with quinoid imine (-N = ), benzenoid imine (NH), and positively charged imine (N+), respectively [32,33]. As -N = and NH are considered to contribute to the pseudo-capacitance, it is beneficial to realize high pseudo-capacitance property of polyaniline by preparing RGO@CN25/PANI8 nanocomposites [34].

The supercapacitive performance of RGO@CN/PANI electrode was investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements in a three-electrode system under the potential windows from −0.7 to 0.3 V with 1 M H2SO4 solution as the electrolyte. Different scanning rates of 2, 5, 10, 20, and 50 mV s−1 were carried out. It can be observed that the CV curves of RGO@CN25/PANI8 have two reversible pairs of peaks, as shown in Fig. 7 (a) . According to the electrochemical performance of PANI, the left peaks can be attributed to the redox transition between insulating leucoemeraldine and conducting protonated emeraldine. And the right peaks are related to the transition between p-benzoquinone and hydroquinone owing to the double electron redox transition [35,36]. The redox peaks of the CV curves demonstrate the existence of PANI component in the RGO@CN25/PANI8 composites. As the scanning rate increases, no severe deformation is observed in CV curves of RGO@CN25/PANI8. It may be profited from the graphene skeleton of the RGO@CN25/PANI8 composites which provides the porous microstructure to promote the fast charge transport and reduce the electrolyte diffusion resistance. Moreover, the CV curves of RGO@CN25/PANI8 illustrates that both the advantage of PANI for increasing the specific capacitance and the forte of graphene for keeping the satisfactory rate performance are successfully realized by the synergistic effect of the components. Fig. 7(b) shows the comparison of several materials at the same scanning rate of 50 mV s−1. No severely deformed CV curves are found in both RGO@CN25/PANI8 and RGO, while the redox peaks of the PANI curves are too screwy to be seen. Meanwhile, the curve of RGO@CN25/PANI8 shows the largest covered area, which means that the specific capacitance of RGO@CN25/PANI8 is much larger than that of graphene and PANI. Based on the CV curves shown in Fig. 7(b), the specific capacitance of RGO@CN25/PANI8, PANI and RGO at the scan rate of 50 mV s−1 are calculated to be 405.3, 236.2 and 130.3 F g−1, respectively, suggesting that the specific capacitance of RGO@CN25/PANI8 is promoted 311% and 172% compared to RGO and PANI, respectively. Combining the analyses of the physical characterization, the improved capacitive performance of RGO@CN25/PANI8 may come from the unique microstructure. The regular in-situ grown PANI nanorod arrays on the graphene surface increases the contact area between PANI components and the electrolyte, resulting in the increased utilization ratio of PANI and better pseudo-capacitive performance. Meanwhile, the graphene component enhances the electrical conductivity of nanocomposites and carbon dots in the nanocomposite improve the capacitive performance of graphene. Hence, the synergy effect of three components leads to the improved supercapacitive performance [37].

To evaluate the electrochemical capacitance and rate capability of the composites, Galvanostatic charge-discharge curves (GCD curves) were measured over the potential window from −0.7 to 0.3 V at different current densities (0.5–10 A g−1), as shown in Fig. 7(c). The GCD curves of RGO@CN25/PANI8 composite are symmetrical, suggesting that the RGO@CN25/PANI8 materials prepared by the supramolecular self-assembly in-situ grown method exhibit outstanding electrochemical reversibility and charge-discharge properties. In addition, the GCD curves of RGO@CN25/PANI8 are approximately liner, and no obvious voltage (IR) drop is observed. The shape of the curves indicates the pseudo-capacitance contribution of PANI component in the RGO@CN25/PANI8 nanocomposites and no obvious IR drops prove the low internal resistance which can be attributed to the microstructure of the nanocomposite and the function of graphene. The regular PANI nanoparticles arrays on the surface of graphene ensure the ample contact with the electrolyte and the improved the ion diffusion from the electrolyte to the electrode interface, resulting in the enhanced utilization ratio of PANI. According to the GCD curves, the specific capacitance of RGO@CN25/PANI8 nanocomposite is calculated to be 620.0, 569.9, 530.4, 501.0, and 473.0 F g−1 at 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10 A g−1, respectively. The capacitance retention ratio of RGO@CN25/PANI8 is 76.7% when the current density increases 20 times, demonstrating the reasonable rate capability. Fig. 7(d) presents the GCD curves of RGO@CN25/PANI8, PANI and RGO at the lower current density of 0.2 A g−1. It can be seen that RGO@CN25/PANI8 exhibits longer discharge time than RGO and PANI, indicating the higher specific capacitance of the nanocomposites (871.8 Fg-1) than that of RGO and PANI. This excellent capacitive performance is comparable with or even better than reported graphene/PANI composites prepared with the addition of linking agents (as listed in Table 1 ).

Fig. 7(e) shows the gravimetric capacitance of RGO@CN25/PANI8, PANI, and RGO at different current densities. It can be found that RGO@CN25/PANI8 composites own the highest specific capacitance and little capacitance loss when the current density increases, illustrating the good capacitive performance. In contrast, although pure PANI obtained high specific capacitance, the practical use of PANI as the supercapacitor electrode material is limited by the serious loss of specific capacitance when current density increases. The improved capacitive performance of RGO@CN25/PANI8 nanocomposites is attributed to the conductive graphene skeleton which exhibits the high stability of double-layer supercapacitors and the regular microstructure which ensures the high specific capacitance of PANI.

To further evaluate the charge transfer behavior at the electrode interface, the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of RGO@CN/PANI nanocomposites, PANI and RGO was tested. From the EIS curves (Nyquist plots), the values of charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the electrodes can be obtained. Generally, Nyquist plots can be divided into two parts: an approximate semicircle part in the high-frequency region and a linear part in the low-frequency region. The diameter of the semicircle represents the charge-transfer resistance (Rct), and the intersection of the semicircle and the x-axis indicate the series resistance that consists of the contact, solution, and intrinsic resistance [41]. While the linear line expresses the collective outcome of leakage resistance, mass capacitance, and Warburg resistance, which is attributed to the ion diffusion or transport in the electrolytes [42]. As shown in Fig. 7(f), no obvious semicircular is observed in the Nyquist plots of RGO@CN25/PANI8 nanocomposites, which suggests a low charge transfer resistance of the electrode. Notably, both RGO and RGO@CN25/PANI8 composites exhibit nearly vertical lines in the low-frequency region, revealing the fine capacitive behavior and fast ion diffusion. It indicates that more PANI participated in the redox reaction in the charge-discharge process and obvious redox couples in the CV curves were obtained under the fast ion diffusion. As contrast, the Nyquist plots of PANI shows the combined curve containing the regular semicircular in the high-frequency region and 45° declining line in the low-frequency region, indicating the unsatisfactory accessibility of the PANI surface for the ion diffusion. To further compare the resistance of electrochemical reactions on the capacitor electrodes, the value of Rct of RGO@CN25/PANI8, RGO, and PANI are carried out as 0.06, 0.7, and 64.0 Ω, respectively. The Rct of RGO@CN25/PANI8 is much lower than that of PANI, indicating that the RGO component has effectively helped to disperse the PANI nanoparticles and improve the kinetic diffusion of the electrolyte ions in the compact electrodes. Besides, the regular growth of PANI nanoparticles and carbon-dots on the RGO surface has also helped to reduce the agglomeration phenomenon of RGO. Hence, the synergistic reaction between three components contributes to the fast diffusion of the electrolyte ions.

The cyclic stability of RGO@CN25/PANI8 composites and PANI was measured by continuously sweeping the CV curves at 30 mV s−1, and the curves of capacity retention vs. cycle are shown in Fig. 8(a) and (b). After 2000 cycles, PANI showed 59.1% degradation of capacitance while that of RGO@CN25/PANI8 composites was only 21.54%, revealing the improved cycling stability of the nanocomposite. In addition, the additional degradation of RGO@CN25/PANI8 is only 5.46% after 3000 more cycles. Keep increasing the cycle numbers to 10000, the capacitance retention of RGO@CN25/PANI8 kept 96.4% in the 5001–10000 cycles. In contrast, the poor cycling stability of PANI is ascribed to the self-swelling and shrinking caused by the redox reactions during the charge-discharge process, which eventually led to the continuous electrode disintegration, less electrical contact between the electrode particles, and apparently capacity loss [38]. To reduce the negative influence of volume change, it is effective to induce the RGO framework to restrict the PANI particles between the graphene layers while RGO also has excellent cycling stability. Hence, the unique microstructure of RGO@CN25/PANI8 in which PANI in-situ grows on both sides of RGO layers contributes to the much-improved cycling performance.

To further explore the effect of additive aniline on the electrochemical properties of RGO@CN25/PANI y composites, the electrochemical characterization of RGO@CN25/PANI y materials with different added aniline amounts was carried out. Fig. 9(a) is the CV curves of RGO@CN25/PANI y nanocomposites, from which the area surrounded by the CV curve increases gradually with an increase of the added aniline amounts from 4 to 8 mmol. When the added amount of aniline was 8 mmol, the covered area of CV curves reached the peak value, and then began to decrease when the amount of aniline was continually increased to 12 mmol. From the GCD curves measured by chronopotentiometry, the specific capacitance of different RGO@CN25/PANI y materials at 0.5 A g−1 was obtained. As shown in line 1 of Fig. 9(b), the capacitance of the composites increased first and then decreased when the amount of aniline increased, which corresponds to the variation of Fig. 9(a). When the aniline dosage is 8 mmol, the specific capacitance of the composite researches the highest value (620.0 F g−1). By analyzing the EIS curves of different RGO@CN25/PANI y nanocomposites, the values of Rct were exhibited in line 2 of Fig. 9(b), from which the Rct increased first with an increase of the added aniline amount and then had the smallest value at the amount of 8 mmol. Fig. 9(c) presents the specific capacitance of RGO@CN25/PANI y at different current densities. No severe loss of specific capacitance was found when the current density increased in five composites, and RGO@CN25/PANI8 showed the highest specific capacitance in different current densities, indicating the best capacitive performance. Based on the above results, the effects of aniline additive on the properties of RGO@CN25/PANI y are summarized in the following aspects: (1) When the amount of aniline is low, the nanoparticle-like morphology of polyaniline cannot be formed on the surface of graphene layers, and the content of aniline is insufficient to develop the pseudo-capacitance of polyaniline. Due to the high content of graphene, the conductive property of the composite is better and the Rct is lower. (2) When aniline is added to graphene-β-CD supramolecular system at the appropriate amount, the synergistic effect of polyaniline and graphene is achieved, and the regular in-situ growth of polyaniline arrays is exhibited, which is conducive to realize the pseudo-capacitive properties of polyaniline and maintain the good conductivity. (3) When the added amount of aniline is higher than 8 mmol, the amorphous thick polymer layer is gradually formed, and the utilization ratio of polyaniline reduced, resulting in the gradually decreased specific capacitance of the composite. In addition, the excess polyaniline also reduces the conductivity of the material. Therefore, the appropriate amount of aniline can realize the synergistic effect between polyaniline and RGO, leading to the composite with the good conductivity and the high specific capacity.

In summary, reduced graphene oxide/carbon dots/polyaniline (RGO@CN x /PANI y ) nanocomposites have been prepared by a supramolecular in-situ self-assembly method in which β-cyclodextrin was served as the linker to form a supramolecular system with both graphene oxide and aniline monomers. In the RGO@CN/PANI nanocomposites, the polyaniline particle arrays grow regularly on the graphene layers, and the surface morphology of the composite can be controlled by adjusting the amount of cyclodextrin and aniline. In the hydrothermal process to reduce the graphene component, the bonding agent cyclodextrin can be carbonized to carbon dots and anchored on the surface of reduced graphene oxide, preventing the agglomeration of graphene layers and providing the double layer capacitance. In addition, the reduced graphene layers inhibit the swelling of polyaniline in the charge and discharge processes, which is beneficial to the performance of polyaniline in the pseudo-capacitance. The prepared nanocomposites have been used as the electrode active material for supercapacitors in a three-electrode system. It is found that when a certain amount of aniline is added, the RGO@CN25/PANI8 nanocomposites have the best capacitive properties (up to 871.8 F g−1 at 0.2 A g−1) and lowest Rct than any single component (RGO or polyaniline). Besides, the cyclic properties of the composites are improved, which present the capacity degradation of only 27% after 10000 cycles, indicating that the composite is a valuable electrode material for supercapacitors. The good properties of RGO@CN/PANI are attributed to the high electrical conductivity of the graphene substrates and the synergistic effect of graphene, carbon dots, and polyaniline under the proper amount of aniline. Since cyclodextrin can also form supramolecular inclusion complexes with many other small molecules, the supramolecular in-situ self-assembly method can be widely used for the preparation of graphene-based composites electrode materials for supercapacitors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to acknowledge the following financial supporters of this work: the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21673086 and 51578556), the Scientific and Technological Plan of Guangdong Province (lithium ion capacitor), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province, China (Grant No. 2015A030313376 and 2015A030308005).
